specification.topic-statement: Interceptable delegating wrappers over the two thread lifecycle primitives — creation and joining — that enable test-time failure injection without changing production behavior.

scope.in-scope: Thread creation and thread join operations as interceptable seams
Build-mode selection between zero-overhead inlining and weak-symbol overridability
scope.boundary: The actual scheduling, stack allocation, and synchronization behaviors of the underlying thread primitives; this module delegates unconditionally and adds nothing
Tool execution workers, wait-command workers, and REPL shutdown logic all use these wrappers but are described elsewhere
Mutex primitives are a separate wrapper set described in the pthread mutex wrapper spec
Override of these seams in test code; this module only provides the seam, not the override

field.name: thread
field.type: pointer to platform thread handle, written on success

field.name: attr
field.type: pointer to platform thread attribute object, or null for defaults

field.name: start_routine
field.type: function pointer accepting a void pointer and returning void pointer

field.name: return
field.type: int, zero on success, nonzero on failure

field.name: thread
field.type: platform thread handle identifying the thread to join

field.name: retval
field.type: pointer to void pointer to receive the thread's return value, or null to discard

field.name: return
field.type: int, zero on success, nonzero on failure

behavior.build-mode-dispatch:
In release builds (NDEBUG defined), both thread wrappers are compiled as static inline functions directly in the header. Each wrapper expands to a single call to the corresponding platform primitive with no overhead. No object file is produced for these wrappers in release mode.
In debug and test builds (NDEBUG not defined), both thread wrappers are compiled as weak symbols in a separate translation unit. The header provides only forward declarations. Because weak symbols can be overridden by a strong symbol with the same name in another translation unit, test code can replace either wrapper with a custom implementation without modifying production sources. In release builds the .c file compiles to an empty translation unit (every definition is inside the NDEBUG-false guard), producing no object code at all.
behavior.thread-create-delegation:
The creation wrapper accepts a pointer to a thread handle, an optional attribute pointer, a start function, and an opaque argument pointer. It delegates immediately to the platform thread creation primitive, passing all four arguments unchanged. The platform's return code is returned directly; zero indicates the thread was created and its handle written to the output parameter. A nonzero return leaves the output handle in an unspecified state.
The wrapper introduces no default attribute; passing null for the attribute parameter causes the platform primitive to apply its own defaults (detach state, stack size, scheduling policy, etc.).
Callers always pass null for the attribute parameter, accepting platform defaults for all thread attributes.
behavior.thread-join-delegation:
The join wrapper accepts a thread handle and an optional pointer for receiving the thread's return value. It delegates immediately to the platform blocking-join primitive. The call blocks until the target thread terminates. The platform result code is returned unchanged.
All callers in the codebase pass null for the return-value pointer, discarding the thread's return value. The wrapper does not enforce this; a non-null pointer is passed through to the platform.
Join is called in three distinct contexts: — Normal tool completion: called after the completion flag is observed to be true, then the mutex-protected running and complete flags are cleared to false. — Interrupted tool completion: called after the interrupt flag is cleared, before discarding the result, then the same flag cleanup follows. — REPL shutdown: called if the tool thread is still marked running at the time the REPL context is destroyed, ensuring the thread is not orphaned at process teardown. There is no timeout on the join call at shutdown; if a background subprocess is permanently blocked, the process will hang indefinitely waiting for the thread.
behavior.coverage-exclusion: In debug builds the entire implementation is marked for coverage exclusion. Because the wrappers are replaced by test-provided overrides during testing, the weak-symbol bodies are never executed under test and produce no coverage data. All lines of each thread wrapper body are excluded from coverage measurement even though they are compiled.
